nodes.label: social-gathering-scent-consciousness
nodes.description: Heightened awareness of personal scent's impact on others during social gatherings with close contact, driving a desire for pleasant, inoffensive, and potentially longer-lasting fragrances in those contexts.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How much do you care about how you smell to others throughout the day? Both

[REAL] When you think about how your scent affects others, is there a particular situation where you're more conscious of it, like at work or social gatherings? Social gatherings with close contact where body odor may bother others

[BRANCH] Which ad are you more likely to engage with, and why? I'd be drawn to Ad A with the celebrity at the luxury party. It makes the product feel special and desirable, which appeals to me for social gatherings...
nodes.predictive_value: Advertising that depicts successful social interactions (parties, dates, group hangouts) and emphasizes a 'safe', 'pleasant', or 'confident' scent will resonate more than solo/self-care focused messaging.

nodes.label: quick-commerce-for-urgency-channel-flexible
nodes.description: Utilizes quick commerce (Blinkit/Zepto) for immediate needs when stock is low, accepting limited selection and sometimes higher prices, but prefers Amazon/Nykaa for planned purchases with wider choice and better deals.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Do you use quick commerce (Blinkit, Zepto, Swiggy Instamart) for personal care? Yes, yes

[REAL] Can you share a recent example where the limited selection on these platforms influenced your choice of personal care product? Bought a smaller skincare unit as a temporary fix; desired product unavailable

[SYNTHETIC] Your regular body wash costs ₹299. During Amazon Great Indian Festival, it's available at ₹199, but delivery will take 5 days. On Blinkit, it's available at full price ₹299 with 10-minute delivery and you're almost out. Buy from Blinkit at ₹299 for instant delivery since I'm almost out.
nodes.predictive_value: For new product launches, being available on quick commerce platforms can capture them in 'replenishment panic' moments, but to secure loyalty, the product must also be listed and well-reviewed on their primary research platforms (Amazon/Nykaa).

nodes.label: natural-claims-skeptic-unless-harm-proven
nodes.description: Is skeptical of 'natural' and 'chemical-free' marketing, viewing them as largely irrelevant unless linked to a specific, known harmful effect; prefers clear certifications (cruelty-free, dermatologist-tested) over vague natural claims.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How important is it to you that a product is 'natural' or 'chemical-free'? {'rating':3,'explanation':'Locally sourced, fresh and homemade'}
[REAL] When it comes to beauty products, do you see a difference between these qualities and being 'chemical-free'? No
[REAL] How do these qualities influence your decision when choosing beauty products if at all? Irrelevant unless a known harmful effect exists
[SYNTHETIC] During a conversation, a friend mentions that most 'natural' body washes in India aren't truly natural... continue as before since the distinction doesn't matter to you.
nodes.predictive_value: A product leading with '100% Natural' or 'Chemical-Free' headlines will be met with indifference or skepticism; they will look past those claims to the ingredient list and specific certifications.

nodes.label: friend-recommendation-trusted-but-researched
nodes.description: Values recommendations from friends and family as a strong initial signal, but will independently research the suggested product online before purchasing, rather than buying based solely on social trust.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What would make you switch away from your current body wash? ... 'friend_rec'
[REAL] Where do you typically discover new personal care products? ... 'friends_family'
[SYNTHETIC] A WhatsApp group of your college friends is buzzing about a new body wash brand... Look it up on Amazon/Nykaa and read reviews from strangers too.
[SYNTHETIC] Two friends independently recommend two different body wash brands... I'd look up both brands online before deciding.
nodes.predictive_value: Word-of-mouth can put a product on their radar, but the product must have a strong online presence with positive reviews to convert that interest into a purchase.

nodes.label: discount-responsive-but-not-at-quality-cost
nodes.description: Actively attracted to discounts and deals, but not if they perceive a compromise on core quality or skin health benefits; prefers straightforward price cuts over complex bundling or cashback.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What type of content makes you most likely to try a new product? Pick top 2: Discount, expert_review
[BRANCH] Rank these five purchase triggers... 2) 50% off sale – a good deal, but I won't compromise on quality.
[SYNTHETIC] Rank these discount presentations from most to least appealing: A) '₹100 off — now ₹199' ...
[SYNTHETIC] If you had to choose between these two deals for body wash... Deal B: Buy 1 body wash (250ml) at ₹259, no freebies.
nodes.predictive_value: A limited-time flash sale or clear price reduction (e.g., '₹100 off') will effectively trigger a trial purchase, especially if paired with an expert review or clinical claim.

nodes.label: fragrance-preference-for-simple-aquatic-gourmand
nodes.description: Gravitates towards ocean/aquatic and sweet/gourmand fragrance families, appreciating scents that are simple, safe (non-offensive), and preferably long-lasting, though expectations for longevity on skin are low.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What fragrance families do you naturally gravitate toward? Pick your top 2: Ocean_aquatic, sweet_gourmand
[REAL] Name a specific product whose fragrance you loved... Titan Skinn Verge. Simple, safe scent; long-lasting
[REAL] How long should a body wash fragrance last on your skin? Shower_only
nodes.predictive_value: A product concept featuring 'fresh aquatic', 'ocean breeze', or 'vanilla/caramel' scent profiles will be more appealing than floral, woody, or musk-heavy options.

nodes.label: mood-dependent-grooming-routine
nodes.description: Their engagement with bathing and grooming varies with their mood, making them receptive to products that promise mood enhancement (energizing, relaxing) depending on the context.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How would you describe your relationship with bathing/grooming? Mood_dependent
[BRANCH] How does your likelihood of engaging with the ad change between these two contexts? When I'm studying, ads are just distractions... At home, I'm in a better mood to explore...
nodes.predictive_value: Contextual advertising (e.g., a 'refreshing morning boost' ad in the AM, a 'calming night routine' ad in the PM) could match their variable mindset and increase engagement.

nodes.label: sustainable-value-premium-limited
nodes.description: Willing to pay a moderate premium (20-40%) for sustainability attributes like eco-friendly packaging and cruelty-free certification, but this is a secondary consideration after core price and efficacy.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Maximum premium (%) you would pay for a product that aligns with your values: {'Sustainable/eco-friendly packaging':'20_40','Cruelty-free, not tested on animals':'20_40'}

[SYNTHETIC] You see a body wash at ₹349 that claims '100% recyclable packaging' and donates ₹10 per bottle to ocean cleanup. A nearly identical body wash without these claims costs ₹269. How much of the ₹80 difference do you feel is justified? About ₹30-40 — I value it but not that much
nodes.predictive_value: Sustainability claims can justify a slight price increase, but the product must first meet all functional criteria (price, lather, skin benefit); it's a tie-breaker, not a primary driver.

nodes.label: time-efficiency-pragmatist
nodes.description: Values innovations that genuinely save time in their daily routine, but is skeptical of shortcuts that compromise effectiveness.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] The most appealing aspect is the promise of getting completely clean in just 60 seconds, which fits well with busy days when I need a quick shower without scrubbing or long rinsing.
[SURVEY] The most appealing part is the 12-hour moisturization straight from the shower, eliminating the need for a separate body lotion, which saves time and effort.
[SURVEY] How likely would the Dove Skip be to save you time in your routine? A: 4
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept promising routine simplification, this person would likely evaluate it against real-world time savings, not just novelty.

nodes.label: conditional-dove-trust-holder
nodes.description: Trusts Dove for gentle, effective cleansing but questions brand extensions that feel gimmicky or misaligned with core values.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How well does the Dove 60-Second Body Spray idea fit with what you expect from the Dove brand? A: Fits very well
[SURVEY] How well does the Dove Skin ID idea fit with what you expect from the Dove brand? A: Fits somewhat well
[SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skip? A: ["Might not work as claimed", "Could irritate my skin"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new Dove concept, this person would likely accept it more readily if framed around proven gentleness and efficacy rather than technological novelty.

nodes.label: proof-requester-before-adoption
nodes.description: Consistently seeks tangible evidence that a product works as claimed, especially for innovative features, before overcoming purchase hesitancy.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skip? A: ["Might not work as claimed"]
[SURVEY] I would want clearer proof that the moisturizing effect truly lasts 12 hours without skin irritation.
[SURVEY] I would want clearer proof that the product truly works for different skin areas without causing irritation.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad with strong claims, this person would likely seek third-party validation, demos, or ingredient proofs before believing them.

nodes.label: value-anchored-at-mid-range
nodes.description: Has a strong internal reference price (₹100-₹150 for basics, up to ₹500 for premium innovations) and judges all concepts against this anchor.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] I would like body washes to... be affordable around ₹100-150...
[SURVEY] I would want the price to be more affordable, possibly closer to mid-range pricing...
[SURVEY] I would make the price more accessible, ideally closer to mid-range pricing around ₹500-600...
[SURVEY] What would you expect to pay for your most preferred concept? A: 499
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new product concept priced above ₹600, this person would likely perceive it as overpriced unless extraordinary, proven benefits are presented.

nodes.label: availability-conscious-shopper
nodes.description: Factors product availability at their regular retail channels (D-Mart, Reliance Smart) heavily into trial likelihood.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? A: ["Might not be available where I shop"]
[SURVEY] Also, availability at common stores like D-Mart and Reliance Smart would improve appeal.
[SURVEY] Wider availability in stores I frequent would help.
nodes.predictive_value: When interested in a new product, this person would likely first check their regular stores before going out of their way to purchase.

nodes.label: multi-brand-rotator-for-daily-essentials
nodes.description: Rotates among several established mass-market brands (Dove, Lux, Pears, Fiama) for daily body wash, showing no rigid loyalty.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Which body wash or shower gel brands have you used in the past 3 months? Select all that apply. A: ["Dove", "Lux", "Pears", "Fiama", "Other brand"]
[SURVEY] Overall, how satisfied are you with the body wash or shower gel you currently use most often? A: Somewhat satisfied
nodes.predictive_value: When targeted by a new mass-market brand, this person would likely be receptive to trying it if it meets their core criteria, as they aren't locked-in.

nodes.label: skin-irritation-hyper-vigilant
nodes.description: Consistently and primarily worries about new products causing skin irritation, indicating sensitive skin or past negative experiences.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? A: ["Could irritate my skin"]
[SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skip? A: ["Could irritate my skin"]
[SURVEY] ...by confirming it does not irritate sensitive skin.
nodes.predictive_value: When evaluating any new personal care product, this person would likely prioritize 'gentle,' 'dermatologically tested,' or 'for sensitive skin' claims.

nodes.label: pragmatic-innovation-assessor
nodes.description: Recognizes and is mildly excited by uniqueness, but ultimately ranks concepts based on tangible, immediate utility in their routine.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How would you describe the Dove Skin ID? ... 'Old-fashioned-Innovative': 5, 'Boring-Exciting': 4
[SURVEY] Now that you've seen all 5 concepts, please rank them from most to least appealing. A: ["Dove 60-Second Body Spray", "Dove Skip", "Dove Night Wash", "Dove Yours & Mine", "Dove Skin ID"]
[SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? 'Innovation / uniqueness': 3
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a highly novel but complex concept (like AI-driven Skin ID), this person would likely admire it but ultimately choose a simpler, time-saving option.

nodes.label: function-over-fragrance-prioritizer
nodes.description: Rates cleansing effectiveness and value for money as most important (5/5), while fragrance is secondary (3/5).
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Cleansing effectiveness": 5, "Fragrance / scent quality": 3, "Value for money": 5}
[SURVEY] I would like body washes to... be non-drying but still clean effectively...
[SURVEY] I would improve the concept by ensuring the fragrance is not overpowering...
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept focused primarily on scent storytelling, this person would likely dismiss it unless it also strongly communicates superior cleansing or moisturizing.

nodes.label: routine-integration-evaluator
nodes.description: Judges new products by how seamlessly they would fit into or enhance their existing daily shower routine.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How well does the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept fit with your current routine? A: 3
[SURVEY] How well does the Dove Skip concept fit with your current routine? A: 3
[SURVEY] The calming ritual aspect... makes the night shower feel special and relaxing.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product requiring a significant routine change (e.g., extra steps), this person would likely reject it unless the benefit is overwhelmingly compelling.

nodes.label: travel-friendly-packaging-requester
nodes.description: Specifically calls out leakproof packaging as a desired feature for convenience during travel.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] I would like body washes to... be affordable around ₹100-150 with leakproof packaging for travel convenience.
nodes.predictive_value: When choosing between two similar products, this person would likely pick the one with more secure, travel-friendly packaging.

nodes.label: texture-specific-preference-holder
nodes.description: Has a clear preference for a watery texture that foams easily directly from the dispenser.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] I would like body washes to have a watery texture that foams easily from the dispenser...
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product advertised as a thick cream or oil, this person would likely assume it's inconvenient or won't rinse clean, and be less interested.

nodes.label: calming-night-ritual-appreciator
nodes.description: Sees value in turning the night shower into a dedicated, stress-reducing ritual with calming ingredients.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] The calming ritual aspect with chamomile and lavender oils that help reduce stress and prepare for sleep is the most appealing...
[SURVEY] Overall, how interested are you in this product concept? (Dove Night Wash) A: 3
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product explicitly marketed for evening use with sleep/relaxation benefits, this person would likely consider it for occasional, intentional use rather than daily.

nodes.label: emotional-connection-via-scent-receptive
nodes.description: Is intrigued by the idea of fragrance creating a subtle, lasting emotional connection with a partner.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] The personalized fragrance quiz and the idea of carrying a subtle trace of each other's scent throughout the day to feel connected even when apart is the most appealing feature.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a couple-focused ad linking scent to togetherness, this person would likely respond more positively than to a generic fragrance ad.

nodes.label: personalization-skeptic-unless-proven
nodes.description: Finds the idea of personalized products (AI skin analysis, fragrance quiz) appealing in theory but doubts its execution and real benefit.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] The most appealing part is the AI skin analysis that customizes the body wash to my unique skin needs...
[SURVEY] I would want clearer proof that the product truly works... and the price should be more affordable...
[SURVEY] ...ensure the personalized fragrance quiz is easy to use and reliable.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a personalized product, this person would likely need a simple, transparent explanation of how the personalization works and what tangible benefit it provides.

nodes.label: daily-shower-routine-anchor
nodes.description: Uses body wash daily, making it a non-negotiable part of their hygiene routine.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How often do you personally use body wash or shower gel? A: Daily
nodes.predictive_value: When shown any new body wash concept, this person would likely evaluate it first on how well it fits into their established daily shower habit.

nodes.label: moderately-satisfied-incumbent-user
nodes.description: Is only somewhat satisfied with their current body wash, indicating a latent openness to switch if a compelling alternative appears.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: Overall, how satisfied are you with the body wash or shower gel you currently use most often? A: Somewhat satisfied
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept with a clear, proven advantage over their current product, this person would be more likely to consider a switch than a highly satisfied user.

nodes.label: pragmatic-brand-switcher
nodes.description: Uses multiple mainstream brands (Dove, Lux, Pears) and shows no strong allegiance, switching based on functional benefits and value.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: Which body wash or shower gel brands have you used in the past 3 months? Select all that apply. A: ["Dove", "Lux", "Pears", "Other brand"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad from a new or competing brand, this person would likely evaluate it on its merits rather than dismiss it due to brand disloyalty.

nodes.label: cleansing-effectiveness-non-negotiable
nodes.description: Rates cleansing effectiveness as the most important factor (5/5), viewing it as the primary job of a body wash.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Cleansing effectiveness": 5}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept that emphasizes novel benefits (e.g., fragrance, mood) without first proving cleansing efficacy, this person would likely be skeptical and dismissive.

nodes.label: value-for-money-maximizer
nodes.description: Equally prioritizes value for money (5/5) with cleansing, actively comparing price to perceived benefits.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Value for money": 5}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a premium-priced concept, this person would likely immediately scrutinize the cost and demand clear justification for the higher price point.

nodes.label: balanced-skin-care-seeker
nodes.description: Seeks a product that is both non-drying and effectively cleansing, valuing moisturization and gentleness highly (4/5).
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: I would like body washes to ... be non-drying but still clean well
[SURVEY] Q: How important are each of these factors... A: {"Moisturization / skin hydration": 4, "Gentleness on skin": 4}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept that promises deep cleansing, this person would likely question if it will strip or dry out their skin, needing reassurance on gentleness.

nodes.label: proof-requiring-skeptic
nodes.description: Consistently doubts that new products will work as claimed and explicitly asks for more evidence before believing benefits.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? A: ["Might not work as claimed"]
[SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: I would want more proof that it works as claimed before committing.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad with a bold claim (e.g., '12-hour moisturization'), this person would likely seek out reviews, trials, or scientific backing before believing it.

nodes.label: affordability-anchor-₹100-150
nodes.description: Has a strong internal reference price for body wash around ₹100-150, judging any concept above this as potentially too high.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: ... be priced affordably around ₹100-150
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new product concept priced at ₹300, this person's first reaction would likely be that it is overpriced, requiring significant added benefit to justify.

nodes.label: watery-foaming-texture-preference
nodes.description: Specifically desires a watery texture that foams easily from the dispenser, indicating a dislike for thick, non-foaming, or hard-to-rinse formulas.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: I would like body washes to have a watery texture that foams easily from the dispenser
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept for a rich, creamy, or oil-based wash, this person would likely perceive it as inconvenient or ineffective at foaming and cleaning.

nodes.label: travel-convenience-packaging-seeker
nodes.description: Explicitly wants leakproof packaging for travel convenience, indicating mobility and portability as a practical concern.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: ... with leakproof packaging for travel convenience.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a body wash in a non-standard or bulky package, this person would likely criticize it for being impractical to carry or store during travel.

nodes.label: familiar-fragrance-preference
nodes.description: Prefers familiar, subtle scents and expresses concern about new concepts having strong or unfamiliar fragrances.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Night Wash concept? A: ... ensure the scent is not too strong or unfamiliar, as I prefer familiar fragrances.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept centered around an exotic or intense new fragrance, this person would likely be hesitant and request options for milder, classic scents.

nodes.label: time-sensitivity-responsive
nodes.description: Is appealed by time-saving benefits that suit busy days, indicating that routine efficiency is a valued commodity.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: The most appealing aspect is the promise of getting completely clean in just 60 seconds, which suits busy days when I have little time between activities.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept that promises to shorten or simplify the shower routine, this person would likely rate it higher, especially if positioned for morning or rushed schedules.

nodes.label: convenience-benefit-appreciator
nodes.description: Values convenience benefits that eliminate steps, like skipping a separate moisturizer, seeing them as genuine time and effort savers.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Skip concept? A: The most appealing part is the convenience of getting 12-hour moisturized skin straight from the shower without needing to apply lotion separately.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a multi-benefit or 2-in-1 product concept, this person would likely be interested if the combined benefit is presented as a true simplification, not just a gimmick.

nodes.label: nighttime-routine-enhancement-seeker
nodes.description: Is attracted to products that transform the night shower into a calming, stress-relieving ritual, showing responsiveness to mood-based benefits.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Night Wash concept? A: I like that it offers a calming, stress-relief benefit with ingredients like chamomile and lavender, making the night shower feel different and relaxing.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept positioned for evening use with relaxation or sleep-aid claims, this person would likely be more receptive than to a generic daily wash.

nodes.label: emotional-personalization-curious
nodes.description: Finds appeal in personalized fragrance linked to emotional connection (like a partner's scent), indicating a responsiveness to products that feel uniquely tailored and meaningful.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Yours & Mine concept? A: The personalized fragrance quiz and the idea of carrying a subtle trace of my partner's scent throughout the day is appealing as it feels emotionally connected.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept that uses personalization to create an emotional or relational benefit (not just functional), this person would likely rate it as more innovative and memorable.

nodes.label: cautiously-interested-in-ai-personalization
nodes.description: Intrigued by AI-driven skin analysis for customization but holds significant concerns about skin irritation and wants proof of efficacy.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Skin ID concept? A: The most appealing part is the AI skin analysis that customizes the body wash for different skin areas...
[SURVEY] Q: What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skin ID? A: ["Could irritate my skin"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a tech-driven, hyper-personalized skincare concept, this person would likely engage with curiosity but demand extensive safety data and transparent ingredient information.

nodes.label: practical-benefit-prioritizer
nodes.description: Ranks concepts with clear, immediate practical benefits (time-saving, relaxation) above those perceived as gimmicky or overly complex.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: Now that you've seen all 5 concepts, please rank them from most to least appealing. Pick your top 3. A: ["Dove 60-Second Body Spray", "Dove Night Wash", "Dove Yours & Mine", "Dove Skip", "Dove Skin ID"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a portfolio of new concepts, this person would likely gravitate toward the one that solves a clear, tangible problem in their daily routine over a more novel but abstract innovation.

nodes.label: innovation-skeptic-not-seeker
nodes.description: Rates innovation/uniqueness as only moderately important (3/5) and perceives all new concepts as only 'somewhat unique,' not groundbreaking.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Innovation / uniqueness": 3}
[SURVEY] Q: How unique or different is the Dove 60-Second Body Spray... A: Somewhat unique (and similar for all others)
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product marketed as 'revolutionary' or 'disruptive,' this person would likely downplay the claim and focus instead on its core functional performance.

nodes.label: brand-trust-is-a-bonus-not-driver
nodes.description: Views brand trust and reputation as only moderately important (3/5), suggesting functional attributes and price outweigh brand equity in decisions.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Brand trust and reputation": 3}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept from a trusted brand like Dove, this person would not give it an automatic pass; they would still critically evaluate its claims and price.

nodes.label: skin-irritation-risk-averse
nodes.description: Expresses specific concern about new products irritating their skin, indicating a cautious approach to formulations with active ingredients or complex profiles.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skin ID? A: ["Could irritate my skin"]
[SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Skin ID concept? A: I would ... want reassurance that the product won't irritate my skin
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept with ingredients like acids, retinoids, or strong fragrances, this person would likely hesitate and look for 'gentle,' 'dermatologically tested,' or 'hypoallergenic' labels.

nodes.label: conditional-novelty-adopter
nodes.description: Open to trying new products only if they meet strict conditions: affordable price, proven claims, familiar sensory experience, and no irritation risk.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Pattern across all concept feedback: consistently asks for lower price and more proof, and prefers familiar fragrances.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown any new product concept, this person would not be an early adopter; they would wait for social proof, discounts, or trial sizes before purchasing.

nodes.label: price-point-realist-₹199-for-premium-benefit
nodes.description: While desiring ₹100-150, is willing to pay up to ₹199 for a concept they find most appealing, indicating a stretch for perceived superior value.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What would you expect to pay for your most preferred concept? (Enter amount in rupees) A: 199
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept priced at ₹199 with a strong value proposition (e.g., time-saving + proven efficacy), this person might consider it a justifiable premium over their ideal price.

nodes.label: routine-fit-assessor
nodes.description: Consistently evaluates how well a new concept fits into their current routine, rating fit as a moderate 3 for most.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How well does the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept fit with your current routine? A: 3 (and similar ratings for other concepts)
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept that requires a significant change in behavior (e.g., a multi-step process), this person would likely rate it low on fit and be less inclined to adopt it.

nodes.label: unimpressed-by-incremental-innovation
nodes.description: Perceives all five novel Dove concepts as only 'somewhat unique,' suggesting a high bar for what they consider truly innovative in the category.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How unique or different is the Dove 60-Second Body Spray compared to other body wash products available today? A: Somewhat unique (and identical rating for all other concepts)
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a line extension or a new variant (e.g., a new scent from an existing range), this person would likely see it as a minor update, not a compelling reason to switch.

nodes.label: happy-with-current-product-inertia
nodes.description: Lists 'Happy with current product' as a concern for trying new concepts, showing an inherent resistance to change that must be overcome.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? A: ["Happy with current product"] (and for 3 other concepts)
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new product ad, this person would need a clear, comparative advantage over their current wash to even pay attention, not just a list of features.

nodes.label: pragmatic-effectiveness-over-brand-loyalty

evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: I would like body washes to have a watery texture that foams easily from the dispenser...
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product demo or description emphasizing a rich, creamy, or oil-based texture, this person would likely perceive it as less appealing or harder to use.

nodes.label: travel-packaging-conscious
nodes.description: Specifically calls out the need for leakproof packaging suitable for travel, indicating mobility and potential product portability as a use case.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: ... come in leakproof packaging suitable for travel...
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product in travel-sized, secure packaging, this person would likely perceive higher practical value and be more inclined to purchase for trips or gym use.

nodes.label: innovation-as-nice-to-have-not-must-have
nodes.description: Rates 'Innovation / uniqueness' as only 3/5 in importance, below core functional attributes, viewing it as a secondary bonus.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Innovation / uniqueness": 3}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a highly innovative but expensive concept, this person would likely reject it in favor of a less novel but more affordable and proven option.

nodes.label: scent-as-emotional-connector
nodes.description: Appreciates fragrance not just for itself but for its ability to create emotional connections and memories, as seen in the 'Yours & Mine' concept.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Yours & Mine concept? A: ... the idea of carrying a subtle trace of my partner's scent to feel connected throughout the day is most appealing.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a fragrance-focused ad that tells a story of connection or memory, this person would likely engage more deeply than with an ad that just lists scent notes.

nodes.label: personalization-curious-but-cautious
nodes.description: Intrigued by personalized skincare (AI analysis in Skin ID) but ranks it last, indicating interest is tempered by higher perceived risk, complexity, or cost.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Skin ID concept? A: The AI skin analysis that customizes the body wash to my specific skin needs...
[SURVEY] Q: Now that you've seen all 5 concepts, please rank them from most to least appealing... A: [..., "Dove Skin ID"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a personalized skincare concept, this person would likely need a very low-risk trial (e.g., free sample, money-back guarantee) to overcome initial hesitation about complexity and efficacy.

nodes.label: step-consolidation-appreciator
nodes.description: Finds value in products that combine benefits (like moisturization) to eliminate a separate step (applying lotion) in their routine.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Skip concept? A: The most appealing part is the 12-hour moisturization straight from the shower, eliminating the need for a separate lotion step.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a '2-in-1' or multi-benefit product claim, this person would likely perceive higher convenience value and be more willing to consider a price premium versus buying two separate products.

nodes.label: broadly-middling-concept-evaluator
nodes.description: Consistently rates new concepts as 'somewhat' relevant, unique, and believable, and interest levels at 3/5, indicating a measured, non-extreme initial reaction to novelty.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How relevant is the Dove 60-Second Body Spray to your personal needs? A: Somewhat relevant
[SURVEY] Q: Overall, how interested are you in this product concept? A: 3
[SURVEY] Q: How would you describe the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? A: {"Not for me-Made for me": 3}
nodes.predictive_value: When first exposed to any new concept, this person's initial response will likely be neutral-to-mildly-positive, requiring further evidence and persuasion to move toward a purchase decision.

nodes.label: purchase-intention-conditional-on-proof
nodes.description: For their most preferred concept, still answers 'Might or might not buy' at the shown price, indicating final decision hinges on post-exposure validation of claims.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: Thinking about the concept you found most appealing — at the price shown, how likely would you be to buy it? A: Might or might not buy
nodes.predictive_value: Even after expressing strong interest, this person would likely not make an impulse purchase; they would seek reviews, samples, or in-store verification before buying.

nodes.label: daily-ritualist-expects-consistent-performance

[SURVEY] Q: How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Cleansing effectiveness": 5, "Moisturization / skin hydration": 4, "Gentleness on skin": 4}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept that disrupts the daily shower routine without a clear, proven benefit, this person would likely reject it in favor of their reliable standard.

nodes.label: price-anchor-at-199-rupees-for-body-wash
nodes.description: Has a specific, low price expectation (₹199) for body wash, viewing anything above as 'too high' for a student budget.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What would you expect to pay for your most preferred concept? (Enter amount in rupees) A: 199

nodes.predictive_value: When shown a premium-priced concept (>₹250), this person would likely dismiss it immediately unless presented with extraordinary, indisputable value proof.

nodes.label: skeptical-of-claims-requires-proof-before-belief

[SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: clearer proof that it works as claimed

nodes.label: functional-innovation-over-sensory-novelty-seeker

[SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: The speed and convenience of getting clean in just 60 seconds... for busy days.

[SURVEY] Q: How well does the Dove Skin ID idea fit with what you expect from the Dove brand? A: Fits somewhat well

evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: have a watery texture that foams easily from the dispenser

[REAL] How would you describe the Dove Skin ID? A: {"Old-fashioned-Innovative": 4}

nodes.label: concept-believability-default-skeptic

[REAL] How believable are the claims made about the Dove Skin ID? A: Somewhat believable

[REAL] How relevant is the Dove Night Wash to your personal needs? A: Somewhat relevant

nodes.label: uniqueness-detector-but-not-differentiator

[REAL] How unique or different is the Dove Skin ID compared to other body wash products available today? A: Somewhat unique

[REAL] Overall, how interested are you in this product concept? A: 3 (for Dove Night Wash)

links.dimension: Preference for aspirational versus relatable advertising content
Credibility attributed to celebrity versus expert endorsements
links.position: High trust in expert endorsements, low in celebrity endorsements

links.mechanism: Because price is their primary decision factor, discounts and flash sales become powerful triggers for trying new products they wouldn't otherwise consider at full price.
Price prioritization leads them to consistently choose basic packaging at lower cost over premium packaging when product performance is similar.
Their trust in expert recommendations makes them willing to pay premiums for clinically proven benefits, viewing expert endorsement as validation of the product's efficacy claims.
Their habit of checking multiple platforms leads them to value high review counts as more reliable social proof than high average ratings from few reviews, which could be manipulated.
Skepticism toward 'natural' marketing claims drives them to verify ingredient lists independently rather than trusting front-label claims.

While their default is to browse online extensively before purchasing, urgent needs force them to use quick commerce with limited selection, creating tension between thorough research and immediate necessity.
Their preference for relatable, everyday scenarios over aspirational marketing aligns with dismissing brand origin as irrelevant gimmickry—they care about practical benefits, not prestige.
Their preference for trying samples before committing reinforces their wait-and-see approach to innovative formats, requiring proof before investment.
Specific skin concern (dryness) makes them particularly responsive to clinically proven moisturizing benefits, justifying higher costs for products addressing this need.
Even when friends recommend products, their default behavior is to verify through independent online research across multiple platforms before trusting the recommendation.

links.dimension: Preference for aspirational versus relatable advertising content
Credibility attributed to celebrity versus expert endorsements

links.mechanism: Trust in clinical expertise drives a more analytical, evidence-based approach to product evaluation, leading them to scrutinize ingredient lists for substantiated benefits or harms rather than relying on marketing claims.

While they prioritize functional lather and skin feel daily, in specific social contexts they become more receptive to aspirational and fragrance-focused messaging, creating a temporary shift in priorities towards impression management.

In urgent situations where immediate need overrides their usual thorough research process, they will temporarily sacrifice price comparison and review-reading for the convenience and speed of quick commerce.

Their responsiveness to discounts makes them more likely to finally purchase a sustainably positioned product at a premium if a deal (e.g., 20% off) brings the effective price closer to their standard range, justifying the extra value.
Their default channel preference (online platforms) directly enables and reinforces their detailed research behavior, as these platforms are designed for easy price comparison and review aggregation.
Their willingness to try new products is conditional on reducing risk, which they achieve by extensively comparing prices and reading reviews across platforms before buying.

links.dimension: Preference for aspirational versus relatable advertising content
Credibility attributed to celebrity versus expert endorsements
links.position: High trust in celebrity/popular endorsements, low in expert endorsements

links.mechanism: The core drive to minimize cost (price-value-calculator) creates tension with the willingness to pay a premium for sustainability. This person will only accept the sustainability premium if it falls within their pre-calculated, narrow acceptable range (20-40%), otherwise, price sensitivity wins.

The belief that brand origin is a gimmick removes a potential justification for price differences. This reinforces their focus on tangible factors like price and functional benefits, as they see no inherent value in 'Imported' or 'Heritage' claims that would warrant paying more.

Both traits stem from a deep skepticism of marketing spin and a search for credible, unbiased information. They trust aggregated community reviews and dermatologist endorsements because both are perceived as having more integrity and less commercial incentive than traditional influencers or brand ads.

Their variable receptiveness based on context directly causes different behaviors on different platforms. When in a relaxed, exploratory mood at home, they engage in deep research on Amazon. When in a time-pressed, urgent need (a specific mood/context), they switch to the convenience-driven, low-research behavior of quick-commerce apps.

Lacking the time or inclination for deep ingredient research, they use dermatologist recommendations and clinical claims as a trusted heuristic or shortcut. This external validation substitutes for their own detailed analysis, allowing them to make a safe choice efficiently.

The preference for simple, functional packaging is a direct extension of their value focus. They view money spent on elaborate packaging as wasted, money that could be saved or spent on the formula itself, thereby supporting their primary goal of maximizing value for money.

Their strong price sensitivity and need for clear value cause them to avoid the financial risk of a full-size purchase of an unknown product. The preference for small trials is a behavioral adaptation to satisfy curiosity and explore new options while minimizing potential monetary loss if the product fails to meet expectations.

links.dimension: Preference for aspirational versus relatable advertising content

Satisfaction with current options creates inertia, making their default purchase intent 'might or might not buy' unless new concepts demonstrate clear superiority on specific pain points.

Their lack of strong brand loyalty is a result of their pragmatic approach; they are willing to switch because they are constantly evaluating products based on core functional and value criteria, not brand attachment.

Their top priority on cleansing effectiveness sets the hierarchy for all other attributes, ensuring that fragrance or other secondary features are always evaluated in the context of whether the core function is delivered.

Their deep-seated priority on value directly manifests as the first and most consistent concern ('price might be too high') when evaluating any new product, setting a initial filter for consideration.
Their skepticism about product claims is the root driver of the second systematic concern ('might not work as claimed'), leading them to demand evidence before believing any benefit.

Their attraction to tangible, time-saving benefits reinforces their overall ranking of concepts, causing them to place functionally innovative products above those with emotional or high-tech personalization.

There is a tension between their initial intrigue at personalized, emotionally resonant concepts and their deep-seated need for proof; the allure of personalization is quickly tempered by skepticism about its actual efficacy.

Their defined price expectation for full-size products makes trial sizes an attractive, lower-risk pathway to try something new without violating their value principles, satisfying both curiosity and caution.

Their meticulous mental calculation of value creates a last-minute hurdle, as they weigh the concrete cost against the still-unproven (in their mind) utility, leading to hesitation and likely cart abandonment.

Their core need for proven cleansing effectiveness drives their broader skepticism; they apply the same evidence standard to the primary function (cleansing) as they do to secondary claims (moisturizing, calming).

While they appreciate time-saving, it's only for specific contexts, indicating they don't want their core routine overhauled. A product promising to replace multiple steps might be seen as disruptive rather than integrative, creating tension.

Their concern for skin safety makes them reliant on familiar, trusted retail channels where they feel they can easily find alternatives or have historically had good experiences, increasing the barrier of a product not being available there.

Their default cautious stance toward any new product makes clinical evidence a necessary key to unlock any level of interest or belief; the two traits form a unified defensive filter against marketing claims.
Using multiple brands suggests that no single product fully satisfies all their needs, leading to moderate overall satisfaction and openness to alternatives.
Because they prioritize value for money, they automatically scrutinize the price of any new product and are hesitant if it seems too high relative to perceived benefits.
Because cleansing effectiveness is a must-have, they prioritize it over secondary attributes like fragrance, which are nice-to-have but not decisive.
Both traits reflect a desire for efficiency and convenience in their routine, whether saving time in the shower or eliminating extra steps afterwards.
Both traits indicate that psychological and emotional benefits resonate with this person, extending beyond functional attributes to personal well-being and relationships.
Skepticism about claims leads to a desire to test the product themselves with minimal financial risk, hence the preference for trial sizes.
Worry about a product not working as claimed makes them inherently skeptical of marketing claims, requiring external validation before believing.
Avoiding skin irritation reinforces their desire for gentle formulations that also provide moisturization to maintain skin health without adverse reactions.
While they appreciate innovation in theory, their caution and risk aversion make them more comfortable with innovations that build on familiar benefits rather than disruptive, unproven technologies.
Trust in the Dove brand creates expectations that new products will align with the brand's established values of gentleness and care; deviations could erode trust.

trait:P15_T013:Concerned about stickiness from moisturizing claims

links.mechanism: Their focus on affordability makes mass retail channels the natural and expected venue for purchase, as these channels typically offer the competitive pricing they require; unavailability there raises both cost and convenience barriers.
Their specific skin concern (dryness) makes them inherently skeptical of any new product's claims, driving a need for evidence that it won't cause irritation before they are willing to try it.

While intrigued by innovation, their demand for proof creates a tension with their price sensitivity: they are unwilling to pay a premium for an unproven benefit, creating a 'prove it first at my price' stance that makes converting them on truly novel, premium products difficult.

Their focus on a fast, efficient routine leads them to prefer a texture that lathers quickly and easily without extra steps (like working up a lather), making lightweight, foaming gels a rational choice for daily use.

Because they prioritize tangible functional benefits in their decision calculus, the emotional equity of a brand holds less weight, leading them to be a multi-brand user who switches based on perceived performance and value.
Using body wash daily makes efficiency and ease of use more important, as small time savings or simplifications compound over frequent use.
Because they prioritize value for money, they have developed specific internal price benchmarks for different product types and actively compare new concepts against them.

While fragrance itself is not a priority, the emotional benefit of connection via personalized scent creates an exception, showing that functional attributes can be overridden by strong emotional drivers.

Although they switch between brands, citing happiness with current product as a concern reveals an underlying inertia; they need a strong incentive to break from a satisfactory routine despite their variety-seeking behavior.
Their default position of finding claims only 'somewhat believable' drives their consistent request for clearer proof before trusting any product promise.
Preference for familiar mass retailers like D-Mart, which typically emphasize value, reinforces price sensitivity, making them more likely to question the cost of premium concepts.
Concern about potential irritation reinforces the importance of gentleness, making them more attentive to claims about skin compatibility and ingredient safety.

Despite rating innovation as low importance, they still recognize and appreciate innovations that solve practical problems (like saving time), indicating that 'useful novelty' is valued even if 'novelty for its own sake' is not.
Because they already use and trust Dove as part of their brand portfolio, they grant Dove a baseline level of credibility for new launches that an unknown brand wouldn't receive.
Because core functional attributes are paramount, innovation is deprioritized; novelty is only considered after the basics of effective and gentle cleansing are guaranteed.

The core drive to maximize value at low price points creates tension with the willingness to pay a premium, requiring the premium product to deliver exceptionally clear and proven superior value to resolve the conflict.
The high concern about skin irritation directly drives the need for clinical evidence to mitigate perceived risk and provide assurance before trying a new product.
The preference for problem-solving innovations makes them particularly receptive to concepts that address the specific, contextual problem of time pressure in their daily life.
The neutral initial evaluation means no concept creates immediate strong conviction, making the final purchase decision highly dependent on gathering additional proof post-exposure.

The daily reliance on body wash for basic cleansing and comfort makes skin irritation a critical risk. Any new product that might disrupt this reliable, non-irritating routine is perceived as a threat to their daily comfort, leading to heightened caution.

The low price anchor makes any premium-priced innovation automatically suspect; the skepticism about claims is the mental justification for not paying more—'if I'm going to pay more, you better prove it's worth it.'

Because they prioritize functional, problem-solving innovations, most new concepts that offer only sensory or marginal improvements fail to meet their threshold for 'better than current.' This reinforces the 'happy with current' stance, as the new product doesn't solve a pressing enough problem to justify the switch cost and risk.

There is a tension between trusting Dove's core equity (gentle, simple moisturization) and being intrigued by a high-tech, personalized extension (Skin ID). The trust provides initial interest, but the brand fit dissonance ('fits somewhat well') amplifies the existing skepticism, preventing full adoption.

Having tried multiple brands, they have a comparative frame of reference. This experience likely leads them to believe that many claims are exaggerated ('they all say similar things'), fueling their general skepticism and demand for proof that a new product is genuinely different.
Because they use body wash daily, any inefficiency or disruption in the routine is magnified, making them highly attuned to and actively seeking out convenience-enhancing features.
Their acute price sensitivity adds a critical layer to their caution, making 'price might be too high' a consistent, non-negotiable check point in their evaluation of any new product.
Because cleansing effectiveness is their top priority, they are driven to seek proof that any new product will deliver on this fundamental promise before considering secondary benefits.

Their inherent need for evidence and verification directly creates the high trust barriers they have; they won't proceed until their proof requirements are met, which manifests as multiple specific concerns.

Their desire for convenience makes them particularly receptive to innovations that promise time-saving or routine simplification, temporarily overriding some skepticism if the time benefit is clear and tangible.
Their active dissatisfaction and brand-switching history indicate a willingness to change, but this is counterbalanced by their caution and multiple concerns, creating a 'seek but verify' loop.

There is an internal tension between their curiosity about new, unique products and their deep-seated need for proof; the curiosity pulls them in, but the skepticism holds them back until evidence is provided.

The emotional appeal and perceived higher value of personalized products create a tension with their strict price anchors, leading to internal conflict where they might consider paying more but demand extreme justification.

Their preference for easy, local availability adds another practical hurdle to trial; if a product isn't where they normally shop, it raises the perceived effort and risk, reinforcing their cautious stance.

While they have a baseline of trust in Dove, it is not strong enough to bypass their need for proof; brand reputation gets the concept in the door, but concrete evidence is required to close the sale.

Their habit of rotating between several established brands demonstrates that no single brand has earned unshakeable loyalty; therefore, trust in a brand is a secondary consideration compared to the perceived performance of a specific product.

Because they prioritize tangible functional outcomes (cleansing, moisturizing), they are inherently suspicious of any claim that might not deliver those outcomes, driving their need for proof before trying.

Their inherent price sensitivity creates tension with their curiosity about personalized, higher-tech products, which typically command a premium. The curiosity is overruled by the price concern, leading to 'interesting but not for me'.

Their baseline skepticism and requirement for proof make it difficult to achieve full confidence, resulting in lingering 'might or might not buy' hesitation even when other factors (like benefit relevance) are positive.

Their preference for practical innovation directly manifests as a strong attraction to the specific innovation of eliminating a routine step (post-shower moisturizing), making Dove Skip their top-ranked concept.
Their vigilant concern about irritation reinforces the importance they place on gentleness as a product attribute, making it a key filter in their decision-making process.

Concern about product availability adds another layer of uncertainty to the purchase decision, contributing to their final 'might or might not buy' stance, as they are unsure if the product will be conveniently obtainable.

Their default stance of 'somewhat believable' towards any claim means that even when they are conceptually open to an emotional/wellness benefit, they immediately apply the same skepticism and demand scientific proof, treating it like a functional claim.
